# The Tony Hawk Paradox

> A nonfiction book by David Borish arguing that video games and simulations
> have repeatedly predicted real-world capability before it existed, built
> into a documented, evidence-based case for simulation theory with
> falsifiable predictions.

The Tony Hawk Paradox is not fiction. The argument centers on a documented
pattern: capabilities appear first in games and simulations, then later in
physical reality. Examples include the 1080-degree skateboard spin (fantasy
in a 1999 video game, first landed in 2012) and Demis Hassabis's description
of DeepMind mastering games before real-world problems. The book extends
this into predictions about AI, robotics, brain-computer interfaces, and
virtual reality, each with an explicit date and falsification condition.

## Author

David Borish is an AI strategist and writer. He developed the Open-Prem
Inflection Point framework and the Exponential Replacement Curve for AI
capability timelines, and has a track record of publicly documented,
validated predictions.

## Links

- Website: https://davidborish.com
- Author writing: https://www.davidborish.com/the-ai-spectator
- Book page: https://tonyhawkparadox.com/

## Note

A release date has not been announced. Visitors can sign up on the book
page to be notified.
